Correct answer: I
5. (i) Conclusion I is C < J; C and J appear separate. So it becomes S = C and S ≥ J. So, these two are our relevant statements to get. R > C ≥ J. This means conclusion I is not true.
(ii) Conclusion II conclusion II is S > N. N appears separately with a common term J; in R < J and S ≥ J. So, these two are our relevant statements and combining them we get R < S. Hence the inference as S > R. Hence, conclusion II follows. Check by Venn diagram.

19.6: Quick method: some final time saving tips
We have already discussed a complete and elaborate strategy for logical reasoning. Initially, the method may look long and cumbersome to you. But after sufficient amount of practice, you may see how easy and quick it actually is. Here we are giving you some additional time saving tips for each of these steps.

Tip 1: First step of decoding should be done on the question sides but careful. Usually, you have enough space in the Question-sheet to make side notes. So, if a stood for 'greater than' then for A to B you should write something like 'above 'α'. So that it looks like A ≥ B. You should do the foolishness of copying the entire questions and then choose alternatives.

Tip 2: Second step of finding out the relevant statements is very important since the point of view of speed. You should (1) understand this procedure clearly and there should be no doubt. If needed, go through Learner's Exercise V again and again; and (2) be able to perform each step mentally and write it in a few seconds. This is very important as well. Please give a lot of practice to this.

Tip 3: In the third step, you first combine the two statements. For this, you are set if a combination is possible using the first and second golden rule. The second golden rule says that a combination is possible only if the common term is greater than (or equal to) one term and less than (or equal to) another term.

You can check this more quickly by following a visual-aid-like common term is greater than (or equal to) one term but less than (or equal to) another term; then the pointed side of the '>' sign (or '<' sign) will be towards the common term in one case and away from a common term in the other. So, a combination is possible in only such cases. In all other cases, the pointed side of the '>' sign (or a '<' sign) will be towards the common term in both the cases or away from the common terms in both the cases.
